main_summary.summary: Joint oral history with Fletcher Anderson and Cynthia Baker Anderson, married Bogalusa, Louisiana civil rights workers and Deacons for Defense participants, recorded by Joe Mosnier for the Library of Congress and Smithsonian NMAAHC. Fletcher Anderson recounts hiring on at the Crown Zellerbach paper mill in 1962, encountering segregated facilities, pay lines, and job categories, and joining Robert "Bob" Hicks and A.Z. Young in workplace lawsuits that opened jobs and supervisory positions for Black employees and Black women. He describes the February 1965 chartering meeting in Bogalusa, attended by Ernest "Chilly Willy" Thomas and Rev. Frederick D. Kirkpatrick of the Jonesboro, Louisiana Deacons, after which Charles R. Sims was elected president, Hicks vice president, and Anderson lieutenant. The Andersons recount marches to Franklinton, the march to Baton Rouge to demand action from Governor John McKeithen, the Meredith March Against Fear protective detail, fundraising trips to California with Lewis Lomax and Dick Gregory, the federal injunction against Bogalusa police, and the negotiation that produced the city's first two Black police officers. Cynthia Anderson describes her Covington teaching job, her arrest on Highway 21 for refusing to say "yes sir," her relationship with Mrs. Hicks and Gayle Jenkins, the Deacons' protection of CORE workers including James Farmer, and women's leadership in Bogalusa.
key_themes: Black women's leadership in the movement
main_summary.historical_significance: This joint interview is a first-person account of the Bogalusa chapter of the Deacons for Defense and Justice from one of its appointed lieutenants and his activist spouse. Fletcher Anderson testifies to the chartering meeting with Jonesboro organizers Ernest "Chilly Willy" Thomas and Rev. Frederick D. Kirkpatrick, names Bogalusa Deacons including Sam Barnes, Reese Perkins, Albert Davis, Willie Jane Preston, Canadian Griffin, and Helen Jefferson, and identifies the negotiation that produced the city's first Black police officers from his own 1959 high school class. He provides operational detail on walkie-talkie networks, block captains, and the armament purchased with funds raised by Lewis Lomax and Dick Gregory in California. Cynthia Anderson supplies counterpoint testimony on women's roles, the Deacons' protective detail for CORE's James Farmer, and the redistricting tactics used after the federal injunction. The interview documents the linkage between Jonesboro and Bogalusa Deacons, the lawsuits that integrated Crown Zellerbach, and the marches to Franklinton and Baton Rouge that pressured Governor John McKeithen to meet with movement leaders.

chapters.title: Bogalusa Setting and Cross-Burning
chapters.summary: Joe Mosnier opens the interview with Fletcher Anderson and Cynthia Baker Anderson in Bogalusa, Louisiana. Fletcher describes the 1963 Ku Klux Klan cross-burning that drew the couple into civil rights activity, the segregated conditions at Crown Zellerbach when he began work there in 1962, and the lawsuits filed alongside Robert "Bob" Hicks that integrated facilities, opened jobs to Black employees and Black women, and produced the first Black supervisors at the plant.
related_events: 1963 Bogalusa cross-burning at Robert Hicks's home

chapters.title: City Hall and Federal Court
chapters.summary: Fletcher recounts pressing the City of Bogalusa to hire Black workers in city government and on garbage trucks, the marches on the courthouse, federal court testimony that produced an injunction against the Bogalusa police department, and his role on the negotiating committee with an AFL-CIO mediator that produced the city's first two Black police officers from his 1959 high school class at Crown Zellerbach.
related_events: first Black Bogalusa police officers hired

chapters.title: Marches to Franklinton and Baton Rouge
chapters.summary: Fletcher describes three marches to Franklinton, Louisiana that drew no response, followed by the longer march to Baton Rouge to demand a meeting with Governor John McKeithen. The governor declined a meeting at the capitol but later flew to Bogalusa for an airport meeting with movement leaders, including Fletcher. He recounts sustained picketing, demonstrations, and economic boycotts that opened retail employment.

chapters.title: Family Backgrounds
chapters.summary: Both speakers describe family histories. Fletcher's grandparents George and Maggie Anderson came from Mississippi to Bogalusa for the paper mill, became property owners, and his grandfather was among the first Black registered voters in Washington Parish. Cynthia describes her father Lewis Baker and mother Esther V. Watson, growing up in the middle quarters of Bogalusa, learning to shoot at age four, and her childhood response to the 1954 Brown v. Board desegregation ruling.

chapters.title: Work at Crown Zellerbach
chapters.summary: Fletcher traces his work history at the Crown Zellerbach paper mill from boxcar loader to truck driver, takeoff person, slitter operator, first helper, and finally overhead crane operator, the position on which he was injured for lack of training and from which he retired. He describes the difficulty of being trained by white workers he would replace and the experience of picketing his own employer before reporting to work the same shift.

chapters.title: Meredith March and Community Defense
chapters.summary: Fletcher describes the Bogalusa Deacons going to Mississippi to protect the Meredith March Against Fear after James Meredith was shot, and how the community organized walkie-talkie networks, block captains, and street blockades to defend Black neighborhoods from drive-by shootings. Cynthia describes the night Fletcher was beaten and the car attacked, retrieving the car from a fire station holding bay by climbing the rope to swing open the bay door.

chapters.title: Chartering the Bogalusa Deacons
chapters.summary: Fletcher and Cynthia recall the February 1965 meeting where Ernest "Chilly Willy" Thomas and Rev. Frederick D. Kirkpatrick came from Jonesboro, Louisiana to explain how they had organized the Deacons for Defense and Justice. From that meeting the Bogalusa chapter chartered, with Charles R. Sims elected president, Robert "Bob" Hicks vice president, and Fletcher Anderson appointed lieutenant. Cynthia recalls suspecting one attendee was an informant.

chapters.title: Deacons Operations and Funding
chapters.summary: Fletcher describes the weapons the Deacons acquired with their own funds and with donations raised on trips to California with Lewis Lomax and others: carbines, thirty-thirty rifles, and handguns. Veterans including Willie Jane Preston who would not formally join the Deacons quietly taught the members organizational and tactical skills. Fletcher names key members: Sam Barnes, Reese Perkins, Albert Davis, and others.
related_events: California fundraising trips for Bogalusa Deacons

chapters.title: Summer 1965 and the Highway Arrest
chapters.summary: Fletcher describes the heightened tension of summer 1965, repeated traffic stops and tickets that drove up insurance rates, and the march to Baton Rouge that produced the first Black state troopers. He recounts a shooting incident during a July 1965 march in which a Klansman attacking the marchers had to be shot, attorneys Lolis Elie and Robert Collins defending the attempted-murder charges that resulted, and Cynthia's separate arrest on Highway 21 for refusing to say "yes sir" to a state trooper, with a $4 bond.

chapters.title: Protecting CORE and Visitors
chapters.summary: Fletcher describes the Deacons' protective detail for CORE leader James Farmer and other visiting civil rights workers, with Canadian Griffin and Fletcher himself assigned to escort them. He recounts Griffin's beating on Columbia Street in front of the Winn-Dixie, the shooting of military veteran Captain Seymour in a phone booth, and the Deacons' overnight protection of marchers in a park in Hammond, Louisiana near Southeastern Louisiana University on the way to Baton Rouge.
related_events: Bogalusa-to-Baton Rouge march overnight in Hammond

chapters.title: School Integration and Aftermath
chapters.summary: Cynthia and Fletcher describe school integration in Bogalusa: an initial 60-40 Black majority enrollment without Black control of principals or administration, the flight of white students to private schools, the retirement or methodological retrenchment of white teachers unwilling to teach Black students, and the disproportionate referral of Black students to special education. Fletcher reflects on Klan surveillance from supervisors at his workplace.

chapters.title: Reflections on Legacy
chapters.summary: Cynthia and Fletcher close with reflections on the Bogalusa movement's lasting effects: pride in working alongside A.Z. Young, Robert "Bob" Hicks, Charles R. Sims, Mrs. Hicks, Gayle Jenkins, and the women who escorted the first Black children into integrated schools; concern about young people's disengagement from civil rights work and drugs in the community; and the assertion that the Bogalusa movement could not have stood without the Deacons' armed self-defense capacity. Fletcher notes Bogalusa was, at the 2011 recording, finally merging its segregated YMCAs.

loc_video.caption: Cynthia Baker Anderson and Fletcher Anderson oral history interview conducted by Joseph Mosnier in Bogalusa, Louisiana, 2011 May 27
